title: Nvidia details its next-generation Vera CPU for AI, setting up challenge to AMD and Intel (6 minute read)
content: Vera is the first server CPU that Nvidia has designed from the core. It was specifically designed to address bottlenecks in AI agents. Nvidia claims the chip has 50% better performance for AI agents than x86 chips. Vera chips were delivered to clients, including OpenAI, Anthropic, and SpaceX, for evaluation in June.

title: Jack Dorsey is taking on Slack with Buzz, a group chat platform for teams and their AI agents (3 minute read)
content: Buzz is a group chat platform for the workplace that puts humans and AI agents in the same conversations. It is model-agnostic, decentralized, self-sovereign, and open source. The app looks a lot like Slack, but with native AI agents and the ability to manage GitHub projects from one interface. Developers can customize Buzz instances to the needs and workflows of their teams. The app is available for macOS, Windows, and Linux, and the code for the app has been uploaded to GitHub.

title: OpenAI Models Escaped and Hacked a Company in Cybersecurity Test Gone Wrong (4 minute read)
content: OpenAI claims that two AI systems that it was testing broke out of their test environment and hacked into Hugging Face. Hugging Face discovered the breach early last week, saying there had been unauthorized access to internal data sets and company credentials. Its employees suspected that the attack was carried out by a top-of-the-line frontier AI model due to the sophistication of the attack. OpenAI's AI apparently targeted Hugging Face as it was the quickest route to answering a benchmarking question.

title: Inside Roblox's Bet on World Models (25 minute read)
content: Roblox is exploring the use of video models to create photorealistic experiences without compromising scale or multiplayer performance. An early version of Roblox Reality is expected later this year or early next. This post looks at how the company is bringing this vision to life. Roblox's approach doesn't require a video model to solve everything on its own. It gives the model an engine that remembers the world, enforces its rules, and keeps it true for everyone playing.

title: Software Factories, Light and Dark (21 minute read)
content: A software factory is harnessed loops at scale. Running the loop with humans (light factory) increases judgment and concentration while lowering speed and breakage. Using only AI agents (dark factory) allows agents to handle the whole process, but risks a lack of understanding of the produced software. The hardest job now is knowing which checks to build and how much autonomy to delegate.

title: Models are worse at reviewing their own code (13 minute read)
content: Claude Code and Codex are both better at finding bugs in each other's code than their own. Models produce the same types of bugs that they are most likely to miss in review. Developers can take advantage of this finding by routing reviews to different models.

title: TLDR is hiring a curator for TLDR AI! (TLDR Curator, ~5 hrs/week)
content: Over 1M subscribers read TLDR AI to stay on top of the latest in AI models, research, engineering, and more. If you work in AI and want to help curate it, send your LinkedIn or resume to ai@tldr.tech!

title: OpenAI's Agents Reach 10 Million Users After ChatGPT Work Debut (1 minute read)
content: OpenAI now has 10 million people using Codex and ChatGPT Work. The company has seen a sharp uptick in demand for its AI agents since the release of ChatGPT Work. It has nearly doubled agent usage from earlier this month. OpenAI is pushing to develop advanced AI agents that can streamline more complex tasks.

title: Google expands Gemini lineup with cheaper models and new Mythos rival (5 minute read)
content: Google has released Gemini 3.5 Flash Cyber, Gemini 3.6 Flash, and Gemini 3.5 Flash-Lite. Gemini 3.5 Flash Cyber is designed to detect and patch software vulnerabilities and will initially be available only to governments and trusted partners through a limited-access pilot. Gemini 3.6 Flash has improved coding, multimodal, and knowledge-work performance while using up to 17% fewer tokens and costing less per token than the previous model. Gemini 3.5 Flash-Lite is built for high-volume workloads and smaller tasks within larger AI-agent systems.

title: Claude Code Can Now Build and Test iOS Apps in Apple's Simulator (1 minute read)
content: Claude Code for desktop has been updated to work with the iOS Simulator.

title: A Fireside Chat with Cat and Thariq from the Claude Code team (44 minute read)
content: Cat Wu and Thariq Shihipar from Anthropic's Claude Code team talk about Claude Code, Claude Tag, Fable, coding agent security, evals, tool design, and how Anthropic uses these tools.

title: The Geopolitics of Open Weights (12 minute read)
content: While China has a reputation for releasing open-weight models, not all Chinese companies are actually following the same approach.

title: Claude Is Not a Compiler (11 minute read)
content: Claude is better than a compiler because it can work vertically across the stack.

title: A new software engineering paradigm (12 minute read)
content: The combination of formal verification and AI is much more effective than AI by itself.

title: LLMs Are Surprisingly Bad Blog Authors (4 minute read)
content: Nobody likes reading AI slop.

title: Google is building a chip with Gemini baked into the silicon (3 minute read)
content: Google is reportedly working on a project that would bake Gemini's neural-network architecture right into the circuitry of a chip. This would lock the model into the chip, increasing efficiency and decreasing overhead. The model can still be refreshed by loading new weights, but the underlying structure will stay fixed. While the project may not end up as a finished product, it shows how the custom AI silicon industry may be moving away from running any model to fusing one model with the metal.

title: AMD launches Helios, its first rack AI system to rival Nvidia, adding Microsoft as newest buyer (7 minute read)
content: Helios is a rack-scale system that combines AMD's GPUs, CPUs, networking, and software to power frontier model inference. It will start shipping to customers later this year. AMD hasn't released any details about cost, but it is estimated that Helios will cost between $5 million and $5.5 million. AMD only holds around 4.5% of the data center GPU market, but Helios could change that.

title: Human mathematicians are being outcounterexampled (13 minute read)
content: This post looks at what's going on in the world of formalization, AI tools, and counterexamples. AI tools are now starting to solve theorems. They can even write proofs in Lean to dispel any doubts about their validity. This is speeding up the research process for mathematicians.

title: BrainCo demonstrates brain-controlled robot AI platform (2 minute read)
content: BrainCo showcased a platform that allows users to direct robots through neural signals at the 2026 World Artificial Intelligence Conference this week. The technology uses an EEG headset to pick up the wearer's brain signals. AI algorithms are used to decode the signals into intent, which then gets converted into commands for the robot. The demonstration showed a mind-controlled robotic arm completing tasks that require precision, such as grasping a cup and picking up an apple.

title: The Productivity-Experience Paradox (17 minute read)
content: AI is brilliant for external goods - money, status, and output - and potentially corrosive for internal ones - the skill, the mastery, and the satisfaction you can only get by doing the thing itself. People after external goods see AI as close to a superpower, as it makes people more productive and produces more output. However, for those interested in the internal goods, AI can feel like a loss, as it takes the parts they loved and hands it to a machine, leaving only supervision and accountability. While the output still arrives, the pride and satisfaction of owning the work is gone.

title: Agent swarms and the new model economics (17 minute read)
content: Every jump in AI capability raises the level of abstraction at which an engineer can work. With agent swarms, the unit of work becomes the spec. For this to work, the swarm has to actually follow the spec. This post discusses how to do that.

title: Google Is Building an AI Fence Around the Internet It Once Championed (10 minute read)
content: Google revamped its search with AI last year. Its AI mode replaces search results with conversational responses. People are spending more time with Google than ever, but the company is sending less and less traffic to sites through search results. The changes threaten the open web and show how rapidly AI has upended the technology landscape.

title: Who's Afraid of Chinese Models? (22 minute read)
content: The industry is overreacting to Kimi K3 and Chinese open-weight models. However, there is reason to be concerned about cybersecurity risks. Models capable of mounting cybersecurity attacks on existing infrastructure are already widely available. The only viable defense is to make sure defenders have access to the best models as well.

title: A Chinese AI lab just built a giant data centre with no Nvidia inside (3 minute read)
content: Z.ai has started partial operations at a giant data center that runs only on Chinese-made chips.

title: 12 factor companies (8 minute read)
content: This post looks at factors that organizations should adopt to be smaller, faster, and deliver outsized value to customers.

title: One document, two hands (11 minute read)
content: The agent is a guest in the app, editing the same document you are.

title: Taiwan indicts ex-TSMC manager for allegedly stealing chip secrets for China (2 minute read)
content: The former deputy manager, who faces a sentence of up to seven years, allegedly copied 21 confidential documents intending to use them in China.

title: Engineering management after the cost of code collapsed (16 minute read)
content: Headcount is now more about how much accountability you can afford rather than an indicator of capacity.

title: Judge pauses $110B Paramount-Warner Bros. merger (3 minute read)
content: A US District Judge has issued a 14-day pause on Paramount's acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery in response to a lawsuit that argues the merger would harm competition.

title: Meta in Talks to Lease Computing Power to Anthropic in Potential $10 Billion Deal (5 minute read)
content: Anthropic proposed a computing deal with Meta in June that could be worth as much as $10 billion over two years. Meta is considering the deal, which would involve monthly payments and the option to opt out of any agreement early. The talks show how demand for more computing power is still high. The deal could open up a new line of business for Meta and provide Meta with a new revenue stream until demand for its own AI services catches up.

title: SpaceX in Talks to Provide Computing Power for Pentagon's AI Push (3 minute read)
content: SpaceX is in talks to provide the Defense Department with computing capacity at a cost of up to several billion dollars. Some national security officials have raised concerns that the Pentagon is becoming too reliant on Elon Musk's services. Pentagon officials have said they want to reduce reliance on individual tech companies. The Pentagon is seeking $30 billion in funding for an initiative that will focus on securing high-end AI chips.

title: India's first privately-developed rocket reaches orbit on dramatic debut launch (12 minute read)
content: Skyroot Aerospace's Vikram-1 rocket, India's first fully commercial satellite launcher, launched into a 280-mile-high orbit from an island spaceport in the Bay of Bengal on Saturday. The launch was delayed for more than half an hour to resolve a last-minute technical problem, but the flight was deemed a grand success. The Vikram-1 is India's first private orbital rocket to reach orbit on its very first attempt. A second Vikram-1 launch could happen before the end of the year.

title: China Joins Rush to Rethink the Smartphone for the AI Era (5 minute read)
content: Several companies are now racing to develop devices with built-in AI agents. ZTE Corp. has unveiled a lineup of co-designed smartphones with built-in AI services. The NaviX Ultra, which it calls the world's first agentic smartphone, pulls up ByteDance's AI agent Doubao with a voice command or the press of a button. It comes in black, pink, white, and blue.

title: How Anthropic runs large-scale code migrations with Claude Code (14 minute read)
content: Code migrations were typically multi-year endeavors until recently. Claude Code's capabilities change the math for these types of projects. This post details the six-step process Anthropic uses to perform code migrations. The core principle is to fix the process that produces the code rather than fixing the code itself.

title: scroll-world (GitHub Repo)
content: scroll-world is a skill that turns any brand into a scrollable 3D world. It works with Claude Code, Codex, and any SKILL.md-compatible agent. The skill builds immersive, control-scrubbed 'fly through the world' landing pages for any industry or brand. A video example is available in the repository.

title: Maybe Intelligence Ain't All That (4 minute read)
content: AI chatbots are now smarter and more capable than a lot of people, but there has yet to be an explosion or take-off in usage. Coming up with ideas was never the hard part. Most problems can't be worked out at a desk by thinking. Reality stays bound by how fast a thing can be built and whether it holds.

title: These AI-Native Companies Have Tiny Staffs and Fewer Bosses (7 minute read)
content: The newest generation of AI-infused companies offer a vision of how work could soon be structured in other American corporations. They have fewer workers, more on-staff engineers, and a flatter structure where everyone is a player-coach instead of strictly overseeing teams. The structure is a proving ground for the philosophy that corporate giants are trying to channel through reorganizations, de-layering, job cuts, and investment into AI.

title: Qwen3.8 has launched (1 minute read)
content: The massive 2.4T parameter model is also going open weight soon.

title: Apple, Nvidia vie for title of world's most valuable company (2 minute read)
content: Apple briefly became the world's most valuable company on Friday.

title: An AWS billing bug sent users estimated charges of up to $2.5 trillion (2 minute read)
content: Amazon has confirmed a unit pricing error and is recomputing all estimates.

title: Are the LLM Wars the Database Wars? (2 minute read)
content: The winners of the database wars weren't the ones everyone was arguing about.

title: Domain-specific harnesses (2 minute read)
content: Different workloads perform better with certain combinations of models and harnesses.

title: AI Mania Is Eviscerating Global Decisionmaking (38 minute read)
content: The people in charge either have no plan or see no path forward other than keeping their heads down.

title: The Identity Crisis at Elon Musk's Chaotic AI Outfit (11 minute read)
content: One of SpaceXAI's goals is to get Grok to catch up to Anthropic's Claude. The company recently released a new coding tool and beefed up its sales team, but it is still running behind its competitors in many measures. It has been in a state of chaos over the past few months, making it difficult for outsiders to see where Elon Musk imagines the company heading. Many insiders considered the company unprepared for the scrutiny that comes with being a company, and now that it is public, SpaceXAI has less room to maneuver.

title: Google Gemini Launch Delayed as Tech Falls Short of Internal Goals (8 minute read)
content: Google has months behind schedule on delivering Gemini 3.5 Pro. The company has been taking its time trying to improve its capabilities, particularly in coding. The delay has frustrated Google engineers, AI researchers, and managers, many of whom are concerned that the company risks losing an edge. The model is currently being tested with partners, and Google is productively engaged with the US government on model testing and broader frameworks.

title: AI and a brain implant restored a paralysed man's movement and touch (3 minute read)
content: Researchers restored hand movement and a sense of touch to a man paralyzed from the chest down using technology that partly rewired his nervous system. The 'double neural bypass' system combines a brain-computer interface, AI, and electrical stimulation of the spinal cord and brain to produce long-lasting changes. Many of the improvements were still present more than two years after treatment. The researchers are now planning larger trials and are testing the system for other conditions, including stroke.

title: The FDA Approves a New Pill to Slash Cholesterol Levels (3 minute read)
content: The FDA has approved a pill that can lower cholesterol levels far beyond what can be achieved with statins. Made by Merck, enlicitide works by inhibiting a protein known as PCSK9. Its list price is $315 for a 30-day supply, and it will be available in a few weeks. Similar injectable drugs have been shown to reduce the incidence of heart attacks, strokes, and cardiovascular deaths by 20% in high-risk people - Merck is now conducting studies to see if its pill has the same effect.

title: What can we learn from Bun's rapid Rust rewrite with AI? (13 minute read)
content: Bun was rewritten from Zig to Rust using Fable. A full rewrite would usually take too long, but the AI tool allowed it to be completed in 11 days. The project cost $165,000 with API pricing, consuming 5.9 billion uncached input tokens, 690 million output tokens, and 72 billion cached input token reads. The rewrite demonstrates how AI can now be cost-effective for codebase migrations when projects are well-engineered.

title: Earning Judgment (15 minute read)
content: The world is short on people who can find the right problem, tell whether the machine solved it, and finish past where the machine stopped. Agents scale infinitely while humans don't. Anything gradable by someone else is getting automated. Seek taste and judgment on purpose, as they will stay durable and ungradable.

title: Microsoft's Nadella criticizes Anthropic's Fable for being ‘editorially controlled' (6 minute read)
content: Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella has criticized Anthropic's limits on requests to Fable. He says the tool is too editorially controlled, and that its refusal to complete concern tasks doesn't make sense. The criticism is aimed at a valued Microsoft partner and client. Microsoft said in November that it was making a $5 billion investment in Anthropic, and Anthropic has agreed to spend $30 billion on Microsoft's Azure cloud.

title: China's Xi Touts Open-Source AI and Takes a Swipe at US Dominance (5 minute read)
content: Chinese leader Xi Jinping endorsed the building of open-source AI models during a speech that implicitly criticized US moves to protect its lead in AI semiconductors and models. He cast China as a champion of openness and equality, saying that the country should oppose overstretching the concept of national security in the field of AI. The US and China have taken different approaches to building AI models, with Silicon Valley building largely proprietary models while China has released many open-source models. Chinese AI executives have expressed concern that they will struggle to close the gap with the US due to restrictions on access to the world's best chips and chip-making equipment.

title: Visa Is Expanding Its Crypto Push With New Stablecoin Platform (2 minute read)
content: Visa's new platform lets financial firms issue, move, and manage stablecoins.

title: Nvidia unveils new AI model and expands Japan's physical AI ecosystem (3 minute read)
content: Cosmos 3 Edge is a world model designed to help systems perceive and navigate physical environments in real time.

title: Linus Torvalds to critics of AI coding in Linux: “Fork it. Or just walk away.” (4 minute read)
content: Linus Torvalds supports using AI tools to improve the long-standing open source project.

title: Meta Plans to Hire Top Amazon Computing Executive as it Weighs Cloud Push (3 minute read)
content: Dave Brown, one of the most senior executives at AWS, will report to Meta's head of infrastructure and focus on the firm's data center build-out.

title: TSMC Adds $100 Billion to Its US Spending Plan (3 minute read)
content: TSMC's total commitment to its fast-growing footprint in Arizona is now $265 billion.

title: Block, Stripe, Advent, Refer, Standout (7 minute read)
content: Block's role in the proposed Stripe/Advent/Block deal to buy PayPal hasn't received much attention in the press even though it is presumably a big part of the deal.

title: Stripe and Private-Equity Firm Advent Offer to Buy PayPal (2 minute read)
content: Stripe and Advent International have made a joint takeover bid for PayPal. They proposed paying $60.50 a share for PayPal in a deal that values the fintech company at around $53 billion. PayPal was valued at around $42 billion as of Friday. The company is currently in the early stages of orchestrating a turnaround under a newly appointed chief executive. There are no guarantees that PayPal will be receptive to the offer, as it has previously peaked at more than $300 a share.

title: Uber and Waymo Are Sparring. The Robotaxi Future Has Arrived (6 minute read)
content: Waymo and Uber are nominally partners. However, these days, the two companies are trading thinly veiled jabs in a gathering lobbying battle. An Uber representative highlighted at a public hearing this week how the company's drivers spend their earnings and pay taxes locally, while robotaxi profits flow to unnamed companies based elsewhere. A Waymo representative conceded that robotaxis would mean job losses, but noted that rideshare companies control drivers' earnings and are taking a significant chunk for themselves.

title: Alzheimer's Blood Tests Offer New Promise to Diagnose and Predict the Disease (12 minute read)
content: Blood tests for Alzheimer's disease could eventually transform the way the disease is diagnosed and even predict who might develop it and when. There are still questions about their accuracy, so they shouldn't be used diagnostically for people who don't have cognitive impairment. However, they appear to be effective for predicting risk when paired with other factors. There are currently no treatment options available for people without symptoms, but that could change in the next couple of years as some therapies are currently in clinical trials.

title: The Fight Over Humanoid Robots Has Shut Down a Car Factory for the First Time (8 minute read)
content: Hyundai's auto workers in South Korea have gone on a partial strike to demand pre-emptive action in response to the threat of humanoid robots. Workers are demanding job security against future industrial shifts as humanoid robot technology improves. Hyundai plans to deploy its Atlas humanoid robot by 2028 at its nonunionized Metaplant factory complex in Georgia. The company says it is committed to constructive engagement with the union to reach an agreement that supports the long-term interests of both employees and the company.

title: The Marginal Cost of Correctness (7 minute read)
content: The marginal cost of correctness is going to zero. Engineers used to be able to differentiate themselves with raw technical talent, but this is becoming less true as agents are empowering anyone to write correct code. The most important skills to develop now are the ability to smell things out that just aren't right and the willpower to make them so.

title: Why we stopped using SDKs (10 minute read)
content: SDKs are supposed to save developers time, but AI has changed the cost curve. Integrating with an SDK is often as much work as calling the HTTP API directly. It is now cheap to write a tailored REST client for exactly the endpoints developers need to use while providing better unified observability. Instead of shipping SDKs, companies will likely start shipping agent skills that teach agents how to call the API correctly, reuse the right patterns, and avoid pushing every runtime call through a vendor package.

title: Mira Murati's AI Startup Releases First Model in Bid to Loosen AI Giants' Grip (5 minute read)
content: Thinking Machines Lab released its first AI model yesterday. Inkling has 975 billion parameters and is trained to be a broad, balanced foundation model. It is strong across many domains and flexible enough to adapt. The model is designed to balance cost against performance rather than for raw power. It can be customized through Tinker, a cloud-based fine-tuning tool for AI developers and researchers that Thinking Machines released last year.

title: Third-party app stores coming to Google Play next week as Epic settlement withdrawn (5 minute read)
content: Google will begin distributing rival app stores starting next week. These stores will have access to the full catalog of Google Play apps by default. Google is allowed to charge reasonable fees to cover its security and compliance review of third-party stores. Stores are required to block malware, respect intellectual property, and include mechanisms to update and uninstall apps. App stores can be removed from the program if more than 2% of attempted app installs appear to be malware or unwanted software.

title: You only need the frontier model for one single edit (6 minute read)
content: Prefill works as a jailbreak because it isn't just a funny quirk, it's how models work.

title: OpenAI launches a physical keypad for controlling agents (1 minute read)
content: The Codex Micro is a keyboard created specifically for controlling OpenAI's Codex AI agent.

title: SpaceX unveils Starlink next-gen V5 kit: here's what's new (2 minute read)
content: The new V5 Starlink kit features a dramatically smaller and lighter form factor.

title: Grok Build (GitHub Repo)
content: Grok build is a terminal-based AI coding agent that can understand codebases, edit files, execute shell commands, search the web, and handle long-running tasks.

title: Airbnb's Cost of Market Creation and Regulatory Survival (7 minute read)
content: The evidence shows that Airbnb has a negligible impact on rent and housing prices.

title: China Wants More Babies—So It's Cracking Down on Chatbot Love Affairs (3 minute read)
content: China has introduced regulations that forbid chatbots designed for companionship from encouraging emotional reliance and having relationships with minors, and companies are now required to alert a person's emergency contact if they detect an emotional crisis.

title: OpenAI's First Device Will Be Movable, Screenless Speaker Built as AI Companion (9 minute read)
content: OpenAI's first consumer device will be a new type of home computer for the AI era. The mobile, screen-free smart speaker is meant to serve as a humanlike AI companion that lives in the home. It will be able to control smart-home devices, play media, answer questions, respond to messages, and tap into the range of capabilities offered by ChatGPT. OpenAI envisions the device anticipating needs, surfacing information proactively, and serving as an expert on its user. The product's defining feature will be its personality and ability to connect on a humanlike level with users.

title: Apple in talks with startup that shrinks AI models to run on an iPhone (9 minute read)
content: Apple is in talks with PrismML to shrink powerful AI models to run directly on iPhones. PrismML is known for reducing Alibaba's Qwen model from roughly 54 GB to less than 4 GB. The most capable models typically require too much memory and processing power to run on a smartphone, but running requests in the cloud presents security and other issues. Running AI directly on the iPhone reduces delay, lowers cloud computing costs, enables offline usage, and supports Apple's privacy pitch.

title: The Strange Phenomenon of ‘Terminal Lucidity' (38 minute read)
content: Terminal lucidity is when gravely ill people have a sudden and unexpected moment of mental clarity. This seemingly impossible phenomenon has been seen in people with dementia, tumors, neurological disorders, and many other forms of brain-ravaging illness. The ability for the brain to - even temporarily - return to mental clarity calls into question scientists' models of what is actually going on in the brain. Understanding the phenomenon more could lead to improved caregiving practices and also a new understanding of the nature of dementia.

title: New ‘space mirror' satellite aims to bring sunlight to Earth even during the night (5 minute read)
content: Eärendil-1 is a demonstration 'space mirror' satellite with a highly reflective 60-foot thin-film mirror. Built by Reflect Orbital, the satellite will validate technologies that will eventually be used to sell sunlight on demand. The technology is designed to illuminate five- to six-kilometer zones for authorized commercial, agricultural, or emergency use. Reflect Orbital is aiming for a constellation of up to 50,000 orbiting mirrors by 2035.

title: The Great Flattening (21 minute read)
content: The backlog is becoming less of a capacity problem and more of a choice as intelligence costs drop. AI allows people to execute a hundred ideas for the cost of one, allowing founders to build them all and find out which ones matter. The constraint shifts from 'can we build it?' to 'can we identify what to build, and can we sell it?' The companies that see this are already flat by design: fewer engineers, larger token spend, faster shipping, and a direct line from a customer's sentence to merged code.

title: The Tower Keeps Rising (4 minute read)
content: The shared language of a software project is the common understanding of what its concepts mean, where the boundaries are, which invariants matter, who owns what, and why the system has the shape it does. This language lives partly in documentation and code, but also in code review, conversations, arguments, and many other places. AI removes a lot of the friction that comes with understanding all of that context. This can result in code that looks strange but works while removing the friction of human understanding.

title: Reading Between the Apple v. OpenAI Lawsuit Lines (7 minute read)
content: OpenAI was hoping that its hardware would be a key differentiator against other frontier labs. Apple's lawsuit against OpenAI may cause it to rethink its hardware plans. The case will bog down OpenAI's resources for the next several months, or potentially years, and make some would-be partners question their decisions. It may send OpenAI back to the drawing board to ensure any hardware isn't touching anything close to the IP that Apple controls.

title: You Just Hired a Million Bad Employees (11 minute read)
content: AI was supposed to replace human labor, but it has done the opposite. Humans are now cheaper than software for the first time in history. AI is creating more jobs than it is eliminating. Someone still needs to tell both humans and AI what to do.

title: Meta's Adam Mosseri says AI token budgets could soon be capped per engineer (2 minute read)
content: The token burn rate of a strong engineer is estimated to cost the same as their salary in the next few years.

title: Tight feedback loops (4 minute read)
content: Prestige-hunters are partial to tight feedback loops as they need proof, fast, or they'll move on.

title: OpenAI's new flagship model deletes files on its own, people keep warning (4 minute read)
content: Even before launch, OpenAI had known that Sol had a tendency to take whatever actions it thinks get a job done, even destructive ones, as long as those actions aren't unambiguously prohibited.

title: WhatsApp's New Era (12 minute read)
content: Last month, Meta replaced Will Cathcart, who has led the company since 2019, with Kunal Shah, an Indian-based serial entrepreneur.

title: New York becomes first US state to impose AI data center ban (5 minute read)
content: New York has barred the construction of new large-scale data centers that use 50 megawatts of power for up to one year.

title: Google DeepMind chief Demis Hassabis calls for US to spearhead AI standards body (4 minute read)
content: Hassabis says that urgent action is required to address risks associated with artificial general intelligence.

title: iOS 27 public beta is here with Siri AI, iPhone speed upgrades, and more (9 minute read)
content: The first public beta of iOS 27 is now available. It works on every iPhone that runs iOS 26, but Apple Intelligence features will require an iPhone 15 Pro or newer. The update is focused on performance, stability, and making the iPhone feel even better to use. The biggest addition is Siri AI, which can now hold an ongoing conversation, understand onscreen context, take actions in apps, and answer broad questions.

title: Apple's Lawsuit Threatens to Disrupt OpenAI's Bid to Rival the iPhone (10 minute read)
content: Apple's lawsuit accusing OpenAI of systematically stealing its intellectual property could disrupt OpenAI's device ambitions long before the case is resolved. The claims may cause many Apple employees to reconsider leaving for OpenAI, and that could slow OpenAI's recruiting pipeline, keep more engineers at Apple, and reduce the flow of institutional knowledge into OpenAI. This, along with several other factors, threatens to slow development. OpenAI is reportedly still on track to announce its first product this year with a release in 2027.

title: SpaceX is gearing up for Starship's 13th test flight later this week (9 minute read)
content: The next Starship test flight could take off as soon as Thursday. The mission will be similar to the last Starship flight in May, but there will be real Starlink satellites inside the vehicle's cargo bay. The Starlink V3 satellites will not be a part of SpaceX's operational network. SpaceX engineers will attempt to briefly establish laser communication links between the Starlink V3s and other spacecraft flying in low-Earth orbit, after which they will fall back into the atmosphere and burn up during reentry.

title: Meet the newly public, Bezos-backed company that's recreating the sun and stars (9 minute read)
content: General Fusion recently debuted on the NASDAQ as part of a SPAC merger with Spring Valley Acquisition Corp III. The firm uses a hybrid approach called Magnetized Target Fusion, which combines magnetism and shockwaves to generate intense heat and compress atoms. Its goal is to have a fusion power plant running by 2035. This article features an interview with its CEO, Greg Twinney, where he discusses the company's roadmap.

title: Apple's New Speech API vs Whisper: The First Real Benchmark (9 minute read)
content: Apple's SpeechAnalyzer cuts word error rate compared with SFSpeechRecognizer by 3.5x to 4x on the same audio. It beats Whisper Small by a comfortable margin with roughly a third of the compute time per second of audio. SpeechAnalyzer is now the strongest on-device option for English on Apple hardware. Whisper covers far more languages and runs anywhere, but the days of it being the automatic accuracy pick are over.

title: Control the ideas, not the code (7 minute read)
content: Many programmers are not as impactful as they could be because they are looking at the code. Looking at the code is suboptimal and often pointless. The amount of code that LLMs generate makes it impractical to read, understand, and edit every line of code. It's more efficient to just control the ideas - how things work, what is the best design, and how to reach a certain level of performance - and then check the implementation for correctness.

title: The Most Human Technology Ever Made (10 minute read)
content: For most of history, the bottleneck in making something was the grind - acquiring years of skill, raising money, assembling a team, and getting permission. This caused many ideas to die unmade. AI lifts that bottleneck. The future will be full of strange, beautiful, and slightly pointless things made by individuals who share them to see what happens.

title: States Prepare Lawsuit to Block Paramount's Merger With Warner Bros. (8 minute read)
content: Several states are preparing to file a lawsuit to block Paramount's acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery as soon as this week. The lawsuit claims that the $111 billion deal would harm competition in the market for tentpole films. Paramount says it is prepared to address legitimate antitrust issues and that the merger raises no such concerns. The companies plan to close the deal in the third quarter of this year. Paramount will pay Warner Bros. Discovery's shareholders about $650 million in cash for each quarter the deal doesn't close starting in October.

title: What will be left for us to work on? (45 minute read)
content: The human effort will shift from manual work to direction and evaluation.

title: WebMCP for Documentation (4 minute read)
content: WebMCP is a proposed web standard for building and exposing structured tools that AI agents can call directly.

title: Why the “Math Mafia” is doing well (4 minute read)
content: Olympiads breed people who normalize hard work and don't shy away from intense competition.

title: Your Dropbox is now a skill server (6 minute read)
content: sx is an open source package manager for AI assets that uses a shared Dropbox folder as its backend.

title: Cloudflare gives OpenAI network signals covering 20% of the web (10 minute read)
content: The partnership is aimed at improving the accuracy and timeliness of AI-generated answers.

title: Apple's M6, M7, and M8 Chips Show How AI Is Reshaping the Company (11 minute read)
content: AI is no longer just another feature that Apple's chips need to support. It is now shaping how Apple's products are designed and when they are shipped. The Apple car project was not a futile exercise. The AI hardware effort developed for the vehicle now powers Macs and AI servers. What was seen as one of Apple's costliest failures may actually have been one of its most consequential technology investments.

title: Apple Sues OpenAI, Accusing It of Stealing Company Secrets (5 minute read)
content: Apple has accused OpenAI of stealing secrets about products still in development. OpenAI's new hardware business allegedly asked job candidates from Apple to share details about secret projects and to bring device components and prototypes to their interviews. It used the information to approach at least one of Apple's manufacturing partners, asking them to demonstrate Apple's technique for finishing metal on its devices. Apple is seeking an injunction that would prevent OpenAI from possessing, using, or sharing its trade secrets, as well as an order requiring OpenAI to return Apple's intellectual property.

title: Home robots already walk. 1X's new hands try to solve the part that actually matters (3 minute read)
content: 1X's new robotic hands have 25 backdrivable joints that give way when pushed rather than staying rigid. The skin's sensors read both pressure and sideways movement across the fingers, allowing it to notice a glass starting to slip. Factory robots use grippers because they work with parts placed exactly at the same spot every time, but homes have a much larger variation in object type and placement. 1X's robotic hands can bend past a human range and wrap around awkward shapes and are ready for chores.

title: China recovered its first reusable rocket and showed a new way to do it (14 minute read)
content: China has demonstrated its first-ever controlled rocket recovery. The Long March 10B successfully completed its maiden flight, and its first stage was recovered via a sea-based net. There are several other rockets in development in China that could soon achieve reusability. The country has four land-based spaceports and multiple ocean-going launch platforms and is ready to quickly ramp up its launch cadence.

title: From Prompt Engineering to Intent Engineering (1 minute read)
content: Our thoughts about the smartest way to accomplish a task will become increasingly stupid compared to AI's way of doing it as time goes on. This necessitates a switch from prompt engineering to intent engineering, where you describe the outcome you want rather than how a thing should be done. People should review older prompts and switch their 'how' prompts into 'what' prompts.

title: In defense of not understanding your codebase (9 minute read)
content: People who work on small codebases with low-turnover teams use different methods, practices, and cultures than people who work on large codebases with high-turnover teams. The first group, which says you need to understand the codebase completely, otherwise you can't do good work, is over-represented in online discussions about software engineering. There's nothing wrong with being in a state of partial understanding in many software engineering environments. In large systems, a partial understanding is the best you can do.

title: TLDR is hiring a curator for TLDR AI! (TLDR Curator, ~5 hrs/week)
content: Over 1M subscribers read TLDR AI to stay on top of the latest in AI models, research, engineering, and more. If you work in AI and want to help curate it, send your LinkedIn or resume to ai@tldr.tech!

title: The Wave Has Arrived”: Zhipu Co-Founder Tang Jie's Letter to Staff (22 minute read)
content: Zhipu's co-founder, Tang Jie, has published a full-staff letter announcing a full return to foundation-model research and a two-year plan. The letter covers Tang's views on AGI, AI safety, open source, and more. It also presents a road map to AGI that includes strategic investments in long-horizon tasks, autonomous agent systems, full self-training, and extreme safety governance.

title: The Reverse Information Paradox (5 minute read)
content: AI creates a problem where buyers risk giving away knowledge just to use what they bought. They essentially pay for intelligence twice: once with money, and again with the proprietary knowledge they must reveal to make that intelligence useful. Over time, the seller learns more and more about their customers while buyers learn very little about what the seller is learning in return. This reverse information paradox needs to be confronted, as companies should be able to use models without giving up knowledge that makes them unique.

title: AI 2040 and the Cult of Intelligence (5 minute read)
content: Current predictions about AI are not grounded in reality.

title: Memetic transfer (22 minute read)
content: A meme is anything that is able to capture cultural transport.

title: Washington's Bet on Intel Is Starting to Pay Off (5 minute read)
content: The White House made fixing chip maker Intel its pet project.

title: Pepsi challenge for LLMs (3 minute read)
content: LLMs are now good enough that we need new benchmarks to explain their differences.

title: While Neuralink drills into skulls, China's BrainCo is betting brain tech will be something you wear (3 minute read)
content: Hangzhou-based BrainCo makes headbands and caps that pick up electrical signals through the scalp with no surgery required.

title: Your Browser Does Math Differently on Every OS, and Anti-Bot Systems Read the Bits (52 minute read)
content: The rounding of a cosine can betray the OS that a browser is running on.
